project.name: TrustAnchor
project.domain: Anti-counterfeit certificate issuance and verification web application
confirmedContext: Used by education institutions and government agencies
Combines digital signatures, double encryption, audit logging, and instant verification
Runs on Next.js App Router with TypeScript
assumptionsToValidate: The forensic inspection metaphor feels credible for education and public-sector users
Public verification may safely show the documented public fields
Bootstrap login opens a platform-admin session, while institution operators sign in with institution-owned credentials

designEvidenceSummary.currentFrontendShape: Small App Router UI with home, login, verification result, and not-found pages

newDirection: warm lab paper, graphite, cyan inspection light, ultraviolet violet, ember seal, and verified green palette
layered evidence sheets with slight rotation and clipped corners
fine registration grids, ruler marks, microprint rails, and clamp-like headers
CSS-only light sweep and paper lift motion with reduced-motion fallback

designPhilosophy: TrustAnchor should feel like a controlled document inspection bench where public proof is placed under light, aligned, checked, and stamped without exposing private internals.

conceptualAnchor.name: Forensic Light Table
conceptualAnchor.specificReferencePoint: A document examiner's illuminated light table with translucent vellum overlays, registration rulers, edge clamps, and UV-reactive security fibers.
conceptualAnchor.rationale: TrustAnchor is about proving that a certificate has not been counterfeited, so layered inspection language fits the product better than generic security dashboards.
conceptualAnchor.signatureMotion: A slow oblique inspection-light sweep crosses specimen surfaces while evidence sheets lift 3px on interaction and status stamps settle into place.
conceptualAnchor.typographicDecision: Condensed technical display headings contrast with readable civic body copy and monospaced microprint proof values.

derivedTokenLogic.typography: Condensed inspection headings, civic sans body copy, compact uppercase evidence labels, and monospaced proof values
derivedTokenLogic.spacing: 10px inspection rhythm with layered sheets, rails, and controlled overlap
derivedTokenLogic.density: Medium-high evidence density with verification-first mobile priority
derivedTokenLogic.colorBehavior: Lab paper and graphite for evidence, cyan for inspection light, ultraviolet for hidden proof, ember for audit seals, and green for verified status
derivedTokenLogic.morphology: Low-radius evidence sheets, clamped headers, ruler rails, clipped corners, and microprint blocks
derivedTokenLogic.motion: Oblique light sweeps, paper lift, control compression, and status stamp settlement using CSS transforms and opacity
derivedTokenLogic.responsiveComposition: Mobile focused evidence stack, tablet two-band layout, desktop layered inspection bench

conceptualAnchor.visualRiskBudget: Expressive layering is allowed, but proof values, form fields, and status text must never be obscured.
conceptualAnchor.motionRiskBudget: CSS-only transform and opacity motion for this slice. Stop non-essential animation under reduced motion.
cohesionChecks: Does the first scan feel like a certificate placed under inspection light?
Are proof values visually distinct from narrative copy?
Do layout overlaps disappear on narrow screens?
Does mobile promote verification before supporting narrative?

derivedTokenLogic.anchorReference: Forensic document light table with vellum overlays, registration rulers, edge clamps, and UV-reactive fibers
derivedTokenLogic.colorDerivationSource: Colors come from lab paper, graphite ink, cyan inspection light, ultraviolet hidden layers, ember seals, and verified green stamps.
derivedTokenLogic.spacingDerivationSource: Spacing follows a 10px inspection-bench rhythm with rails, margins, overlays, and readable evidence labels.
derivedTokenLogic.typographyDerivationSource: Typography separates technical inspection headings, plain institutional body copy, and monospaced microprint proof values.
derivedTokenLogic.motionDerivationSource: Motion imitates oblique light sweeps, paper lift, button compression, and status stamp settlement.
derivedTokenLogic.validationRule: Every visual token must explain how it supports document inspection rather than generic dashboard presentation.

libraryDecisions.name: qrcode-generator
libraryDecisions.purpose: Render scannable verification QR matrices as SVG without a binary runtime dependency
libraryDecisions.risk: The Docker node_modules volume must be refreshed after package changes
libraryDecisions.fallbackIfUnavailable: Keep verification code text visible and defer QR rendering until dependency install succeeds

libraryDecisions.name: No new animation, icon, canvas, or UI primitive library
libraryDecisions.purpose: Keep certificate print output within the existing Next.js, React, CSS, and form stack
libraryDecisions.risk: CSS must stay organized because no component library is hiding complexity
libraryDecisions.fallbackIfUnavailable: Native CSS and existing React components

visualDirection.name: Forensic evidence bench
distinctiveMoves: Landing page uses layered document sheets as the primary visual asset
Verification console is positioned like a lit evidence slip
Trust chain appears as numbered inspection passes instead of equal cards
Verification result becomes a stamped public evidence sheet
Print-ready certificate artifact becomes a formal evidence sheet with hash strip and QR verification link

mutationRules.mobile: Verification slip appears first; document specimen and proof passes stack below it with no rotation or overlap.
mutationRules.tablet: Verification and specimen form two stacked bands; secondary evidence sections may pair into two columns.
mutationRules.desktop: Use a wide layered inspection bench with verification, specimen, and proof rail visible together.

motionSystem.dependencyDecision: No new animation dependency for this slice
motionSystem.approach: CSS-only transforms, opacity, pseudo-element light sweeps, paper lift, and status stamp settlement

identityRules: Primary actions read as illuminated controls
Evidence surfaces use clipped sheet corners and registration rails
Proof blocks use monospaced alignment and stronger containment than narrative blocks
Status chips include readable text and may not rely on color alone

reviewRubric.distinctiveness: The page should read as TrustAnchor's forensic evidence bench, not a generic SaaS shell.
reviewRubric.contractFidelity: Layered sheets, proof rails, typography, color, and responsive order must derive from the light-table anchor.
reviewRubric.hierarchy: Verification must be the most obvious first action.
reviewRubric.responsiveRecomposition: Mobile, tablet, and desktop must change grouping and priority.
reviewRubric.motionDiscipline: Motion must support inspection or state feedback and stop under reduced motion.
reviewRubric.accessibility: Focus, contrast, target size, form errors, and status text must remain intact.

forbiddenPatterns: observatory signal rings from the previous concept
decorative animation that hides the verification task
proof strings styled like marketing copy
nextValidationAction: Validate the forensic light-table redesign with API smoke, typecheck, lint, responsive inspection, reduced-motion behavior, and the project PR checklist.
